papers.title: Autonomous Radiotherapy Treatment Planning Using DOLA: A Privacy-Preserving, LLM-Based Optimization Agent
papers.abstract: Radiotherapy treatment planning is a complex and time-intensive process, often impacted by inter-planner variability and subjective decision-making. To address these challenges, we introduce Dose Optimization Language Agent (DOLA), an autonomous large language model (LLM)-based agent designed for optimizing radiotherapy treatment plans while rigorously protecting patient privacy. DOLA integrates the LLaMa3.1 LLM directly with a commercial treatment planning system, utilizing chain-of-thought prompting, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and reinforcement learning (RL). Operating entirely within secure local infrastructure, this agent eliminates external data sharing. We evaluated DOLA using a retrospective cohort of 18 prostate cancer patients prescribed 60 Gy in 20 fractions, comparing model sizes (8 billion vs. 70 billion parameters) and optimization strategies (No-RAG, RAG, and RAG+RL) over 10 planning iterations. The 70B model demonstrated significantly improved performance, achieving approximately 16.4% higher final scores than the 8B model. The RAG approach outperformed the No-RAG baseline by 19.8%, and incorporating RL accelerated convergence, highlighting the synergy of retrieval-based memory and reinforcement learning. Optimal temperature hyperparameter analysis identified 0.4 as providing the best balance between exploration and exploitation. This proof of concept study represents the first successful deployment of locally hosted LLM agents for autonomous optimization of treatment plans within a commercial radiotherapy planning system. By extending human-machine interaction through interpretable natural language reasoning, DOLA offers a scalable and privacy-conscious framework, with significant potential for clinical implementation and workflow improvement.

papers.title: Zero-Shot Reinforcement Learning Under Partial Observability
papers.abstract: Recent work has shown that, under certain assumptions, zero-shot reinforcement learning (RL) methods can generalise to any unseen task in an environment after reward-free pre-training. Access to Markov states is one such assumption, yet, in many real-world applications, the Markov state is only partially observable. Here, we explore how the performance of standard zero-shot RL methods degrades when subjected to partially observability, and show that, as in single-task RL, memory-based architectures are an effective remedy. We evaluate our memory-based zero-shot RL methods in domains where the states, rewards and a change in dynamics are partially observed, and show improved performance over memory-free baselines. Our code is open-sourced via: https://enjeeneer.io/projects/bfms-with-memory/.

These are fundamentally different research questions with distinct application domains and goals. | Method: Both the user's idea and the paper utilize reinforcement learning. However, the paper focuses on zero-shot RL under partial observability using memory-based architectures like RNNs, GRUs, and transformers for generalisation to unseen tasks, as stated in the abstract and introduction. The user's idea is about personalized adaptive radiotherapy treatment planning, which is a specific application, and does not mention zero-shot learning or partial observability as a core methodological challenge, but rather iterative adjustment based on simulated biological dynamics. | Domain: The user's research idea is in personalized adaptive radiotherapy treatment planning, a specific medical application. The paper, however, focuses on theoretical aspects of zero-shot reinforcement learning under partial observability, evaluating its methods in general domains without specifying any particular application, especially not in healthcare or radiotherapy. | Contribution: The paper focuses on addressing partial observability in zero-shot reinforcement learning using memory-based architectures, evaluating performance degradation and remedies in general RL domains. The user's idea, in contrast, proposes a reinforcement learning agent for personalized adaptive radiotherapy treatment planning, specifically focusing on iteratively adjusting dose fractionation based on simulated patient-specific biological dynamics. These are distinct application areas and research contributions.

papers.title: Physics-Guided Radiotherapy Treatment Planning with Deep Learning
papers.abstract: Radiotherapy (RT) is a critical cancer treatment, with volumetric modulated arc therapy (VMAT) being a commonly used technique that enhances dose conformity by dynamically adjusting multileaf collimator (MLC) positions and monitor units (MU) throughout gantry rotation. Adaptive radiotherapy requires frequent modifications to treatment plans to account for anatomical variations, necessitating time-efficient solutions. Deep learning offers a promising solution to automate this process. To this end, we propose a two-stage, physics-guided deep learning pipeline for radiotherapy planning. In the first stage, our network is trained with direct supervision on treatment plan parameters, consisting of MLC and MU values. In the second stage, we incorporate an additional supervision signal derived from the predicted 3D dose distribution, integrating physics-based guidance into the training process. We train and evaluate our approach on 133 prostate cancer patients treated with a uniform 2-arc VMAT protocol delivering a dose of 62 Gy to the planning target volume (PTV). Our results demonstrate that the proposed approach, implemented using both 3D U-Net and UNETR architectures, consistently produces treatment plans that closely match clinical ground truths. Our method achieves a mean difference of D95% = 0.42 +/- 1.83 Gy and V95% = -0.22 +/- 1.87% at the PTV while generating dose distributions that reduce radiation exposure to organs at risk. These findings highlight the potential of physics-guided deep learning in RT planning.

In contrast, the user's idea proposes a reinforcement learning agent that iteratively adjusts dose fractionation based on simulated tumor and healthy tissue dynamics, which is a fundamentally different approach to treatment plan optimization and personalization.

papers.title: Offline Reinforcement Learning from Datasets with Structured Non-Stationarity
papers.abstract: Current Reinforcement Learning (RL) is often limited by the large amount of data needed to learn a successful policy. Offline RL aims to solve this issue by using transitions collected by a different behavior policy. We address a novel Offline RL problem setting in which, while collecting the dataset, the transition and reward functions gradually change between episodes but stay constant within each episode. We propose a method based on Contrastive Predictive Coding that identifies this non-stationarity in the offline dataset, accounts for it when training a policy, and predicts it during evaluation. We analyze our proposed method and show that it performs well in simple continuous control tasks and challenging, high-dimensional locomotion tasks. We show that our method often achieves the oracle performance and performs better than baselines.

papers.title: A Digital Twin Framework for Adaptive Treatment Planning in Radiotherapy
papers.abstract: The development of a digital twin (DT) framework for fast online adaptive proton therapy planning in prostate stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) with dominant intraprostatic lesion (DIL) boost represents a significant advancement in personalized radiotherapy. This framework integrates deep learning-based multi-atlas deformable image registration, daily patient anatomy updates via cone-beam CT (CBCT), and knowledge-based plan quality evaluation using the ProKnow scoring system to achieve clinical-equivalent plan quality with substantially reduced reoptimization times compared to traditional clinical workflows. Drawing on a database of 43 prior prostate SBRT cases, the DT framework predicts interfractional anatomical variations for new patients and pre-generates multiple probabilistic treatment plans. Upon acquiring daily CBCT, it enables rapid plan reoptimization, achieving an average reoptimization time of 5.5 [2.8, 8.2] minutes, compared to 19.8 [7.9, 31.7] minutes for clinical plans. The DT-based plans yielded a plan quality score of 157.2 [151.6, 162.8], surpassing or matching clinical plans, with superior dose coverage for the DIL (V100: 99.5%) and clinical target volume (CTV V100: 99.8%). Additionally, the framework minimized doses to organs at risk (OARs), achieving bladder V20.8Gy of 11.4 [7.2, 15.6] cc, rectum V23Gy of 0.7 [0.3, 1.1] cc, and urethra D10 of 90.9% [88.6%, 93.2%], aligning with clinical standards. By addressing interfractional variations efficiently, the DT framework enhances treatment precision, reduces OAR toxicity, and supports real-time adaptive radiotherapy. This transformative approach not only streamlines the planning process but also improves clinical outcomes, offering a scalable solution for prostate SBRT with DIL boost and paving the way for broader applications in adaptive proton therapy.
papers.reason: Problem: The paper addresses the problem of enhancing adaptive radiotherapy planning through a digital twin framework to improve precision and reduce reoptimization times, particularly for prostate SBRT with DIL boost. The paper focuses on 'adaptive proton therapy planning in prostate stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT)' and 'online adaptive radiotherapy', which is a closely related application target to the user's idea of 'personalized adaptive radiotherapy treatment plans'. The paper also mentions 'Reinforcement learning (RL) (Zhang et al. , 2021) and deep reinforcement learning (DRL) show promise for photon-based prostate cancer treatment planning', directly linking to the user's proposed 'reinforcement learning agent'. | Contribution: The paper's digital twin framework focuses on rapid online adaptive radiotherapy planning by addressing anatomical variations and pre-generating probabilistic plans, as stated in 'Fast Online Plan Reoptimization' and 'Efficient Plan Selection and Tissue Sparing'. While it mentions reinforcement learning as a promising future direction for plan optimization, it does not claim to use RL to iteratively adjust dose fractionation based on tumor response and healthy tissue recovery dynamics, which is a core contribution of the user's idea.

existing_examples.name: Adaptive Radiotherapy (ART)
existing_examples.description: ART is an established clinical concept where treatment plans are modified during the course of treatment based on changes in patient anatomy (e.g., tumor shrinkage, weight loss). While often driven by imaging and re-planning, the core idea of adapting to patient changes is present.

existing_examples.name: AI/ML in Radiotherapy Planning Research
existing_examples.description: There is extensive research on using machine learning, including deep learning and reinforcement learning, for various aspects of radiotherapy, such as auto-segmentation, dose prediction, and treatment plan optimization. Many studies explore RL for dynamic treatment adaptation, though often in simulated environments or for specific sub-problems.

existing_examples.name: Biological/Personalized Radiotherapy Research
existing_examples.description: Research efforts are ongoing to incorporate patient-specific biological data (e.g., genomics, radiomics, tumor microenvironment) into radiotherapy planning to personalize treatments beyond anatomical considerations. This often involves modeling tumor response and normal tissue toxicity.

existing_examples.name: In-silico Clinical Trials/Simulations
existing_examples.description: The use of computational models to simulate tumor growth, response to radiation, and normal tissue toxicity is a well-established research area, providing the 'simulated tumor response and healthy tissue recovery dynamics' mentioned in the idea.

reality_check.assessment: The core components of this idea – adaptive radiotherapy, personalized treatment, and the application of reinforcement learning for optimization – are all active and well-established areas of research and, to some extent, clinical practice. While a fully autonomous RL agent designing and iteratively adjusting dose fractionation based on complex patient-specific biological models in a real-time clinical setting might not be a widely deployed 'product' yet, the underlying research and technological building blocks are very much in existence and under active development. Many research groups are exploring similar concepts, often in simulation.
novelty_aspects: The specific integration and real-world deployment of a *fully autonomous* RL agent for *iterative dose fractionation adjustment* based on *patient-specific biological dynamics* in a clinical workflow might still represent a significant engineering and validation challenge, even if the individual components exist.
The granularity and sophistication of the 'patient-specific biology models' and their real-time integration with an RL agent could offer novelty if significantly advanced beyond current capabilities.
reality_check.recommendation: While the general concept is not novel, there is still significant room for innovation in the specific implementation, validation, and clinical translation. Focus on defining the unique aspects of your approach: What specific biological models will you use? How will you handle the complexity and uncertainty of patient-specific data? What RL algorithms will be most effective? How will you validate the safety and efficacy of such an agent in a clinical setting? Consider a specific niche or a significant improvement over existing research rather than the broad concept.

matched_sections.paper_title: Zero-Shot Reinforcement Learning Under Partial Observability
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: These results have assumed access to Markov states that provide all the information the agent requires to solve a task. Though this is a common assumption in RL, for many interesting problems, the Markov state is only partially observed via unreliable or incomplete observations (Kaelbling et al., 1998).

matched_sections.paper_title: Offline Reinforcement Learning from Datasets with Structured Non-Stationarity
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: Amain challenge of Reinforcement Learning (RL) is the large amount of interactions required to learn a proficient policy. One recently popular way to tackle this challenge is to use Offline Reinforcement Learning (Levine et al., 2020). In Offline RL we aim to learn a policy from a given dataset of previous transitions generated by a different behavior policy, without needing to interact with the environment further.
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper explicitly mention 'Reinforcement Learning' and the goal of learning a 'policy' or 'agent' to make decisions. The paper focuses on Offline RL, which is a specific type of RL, while the user's idea uses a general 'reinforcement learning agent'.

matched_sections.paper_title: Offline Reinforcement Learning from Datasets with Structured Non-Stationarity
matched_sections.heading: Reinforcement Learning
matched_sections.text_snippet: In RL, we are given an MDP M = ( S, A, R, p, p 0 ) with, in this work, continuous state space S , continuous action space A , deterministic reward function R ( s, a ), transition probability density p ( s ′ | s, a ) and initial state density p 0 ( s ) (Sutton & Barto, 2018). We then aim to learn a policy with conditional probability density π ( a | s ) of choosing action a in state s , that maximizes the expected return J = E [ ∑ H t =0 r t ] , where H is the duration of an episode.
matched_sections.reason: This section defines Reinforcement Learning (RL) and the goal of learning a policy to maximize expected return, which aligns with the user's proposal of a 'reinforcement learning agent that learns to design personalized adaptive radiotherapy treatment plans'.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Digital Twin Framework for Adaptive Treatment Planning in Radiotherapy
matched_sections.heading: Discussion
matched_sections.text_snippet: The overarching objective of the DT framework is to achieve fast personalized radiation therapy, raising two critical questions to enhance its practical feasibility: (1) How can online plan reoptimization be efficiently and reliably performed within 10 minutes using automation to improve accuracy and consistency? (2) How can the inherent uncertainty in patient anatomy be systematically and effectively quantified to inform treatment adjustments?
matched_sections.reason: The paper discusses the problem of achieving fast personalized radiation therapy and the need for efficient online plan reoptimization and quantifying anatomical uncertainty, which aligns with the user's goal of designing personalized adaptive radiotherapy treatment plans.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Digital Twin Framework for Adaptive Treatment Planning in Radiotherapy
matched_sections.heading: Discussion
matched_sections.text_snippet: Reinforcement learning (RL) (Zhang et al. , 2021) and deep reinforcement learning (DRL) show promise for photon-based prostate cancer treatment planning, as demonstrated by recent studies (Hrinivich and Lee, 2020; Shen et al. , 2020; Shen et al. , 2021). However, implementing DRL within a commercial treatment planning system can require up to 2 hours for plan optimization (Gao et al. , 2023). With effective initial plan conditions, Hrinivich et al. (Hrinivich et al. , 2024) showed that DRL can rapidly generate plans for photon-based prostate cancer treatment planning.
matched_sections.reason: The user explicitly proposes a 'reinforcement learning agent'. The paper discusses the promise of RL and DRL for treatment planning, indicating a direct methodological overlap.

linked_sections.paper_title: Autonomous Radiotherapy Treatment Planning Using DOLA: A Privacy-Preserving, LLM-Based Optimization Agent
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: Radiotherapy treatment planning is a complex and time-intensive process, often impacted by inter-planner variability and subjective decision-making. To address these challenges, we introduce Dose Optimization Language Agent (DOLA), an autonomous large language model (LLM)-based agent designed for optimizing radiotherapy treatment plans while rigorously protecting patient privacy.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper address the problem of optimizing radiotherapy treatment plans. The paper focuses on variability and privacy, while the user's idea emphasizes personalization and adaptive aspects, which is a slightly different angle on the problem.

linked_sections.paper_title: Autonomous Radiotherapy Treatment Planning Using DOLA: A Privacy-Preserving, LLM-Based Optimization Agent
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: DOLA integrates the LLaMa3.1 LLM directly with a commercial treatment planning system, utilizing chain-of-thought prompting, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and reinforcement learning (RL).
linked_sections.reason: The user's idea explicitly mentions a 'reinforcement learning agent' for designing treatment plans. The paper also uses 'reinforcement learning (RL)' as a key component of its methodology for optimizing radiotherapy treatment plans.
